summary: My name is Kwame Wood, but I perform under the stage name Rasskwame. I was born in Accra, Ghana, in 1963, and music has been my passion since I was nine years old when I joined a student band that won a national contest. Over the years, I have toured, recorded albums, and formed my own band called Culture Shock. Despite challenges as an independent artist in the United States, I continue to compose, arrange, and produce my own music, which focuses on uplifting the oppressed and honoring my heritage.

I was born in Accra, the capital city of Ghana, in September 1963. Growing up there in West Africa shaped much of my early musical influences and ambitions. It was in Accra that I joined a student band that won a national contest in 1977, which set me on the path to becoming a professional musician. Though I later moved to the United States, Accra remains an important part of my identity and musical roots.

Ghana is my homeland, where I was born and began my musical journey. In 1986, I met the famous Ghanaian musician C.K. Mann, who became a mentor and invited me to tour North America in 1990. Although the tour faced visa issues, Ghana remains central to my music and message, which often pays homage to my ancestors and the unity of Africa. I have had the chance to visit Ghana twice since moving abroad, and it continues to inspire my work.

I was born in West Africa, specifically in Accra, Ghana, in 1963. Growing up in this region influenced my musical style and the themes I explore in my songs, such as African unity and heritage. My music carries the spirit of West African culture, blending reggae and world beat to uplift and connect people. Even after relocating to the United States, my roots in West Africa remain a vital part of who I am.

In 1986, during one of my band's performances in Ghana, I met C.K. Mann, a famous musician who promised to tour with me one day. True to his word, he invited me to join him on a North American tour in 1990. Although visa delays prevented us from performing as planned, meeting C.K. Mann was a pivotal moment in my career and helped me transition to life and music in the United States.

When I arrived in New York City in 1990 after the visa issues disrupted our tour, I faced a tough culture shock. I initially drove a taxi cab to make ends meet before joining a band in the city. New York was my first home in the United States and a place where I adapted to a new life while continuing my passion for music.

After spending five years in New York City, I relocated to Washington, D.C., where I formed my own band called Culture Shock. It was in D.C. that I recorded my first album, Ancestors, marking an important milestone in my career. The city provided a supportive environment for my music, which blends reggae and world beat with messages about African heritage and unity.

Culture Shock is the band I formed in Washington, D.C. after leaving New York City. Through Culture Shock, I was able to express my musical vision, blending reggae and world beat with themes of African unity and honoring ancestors. This band was a key part of my growth as an independent artist in the United States.

Ancestors is my first album, which I recorded in Washington, D.C. after forming Culture Shock. Released in the mid-1990s, this album reflects my deep respect for my heritage and the struggles of the oppressed. It was a significant step in establishing my voice as a musician in the United States.

In 1996, I met my children's mother, who is from Minneapolis, and in 1997 I relocated there to build our family life. Minneapolis became my home where I raised our three children and continued my music career. It was also where I recorded my second album, Ananse Stories, in 2010 and became a U.S. citizen that same year.

Ananse Stories is my second album, recorded in 2010 while I was living in Minneapolis. This album continues my tradition of blending reggae and world beat with storytelling rooted in African folklore and culture. It reflects my ongoing commitment to honoring my ancestors and sharing African narratives through music.

I have lived in the United States since 1990, starting in New York City and later moving to Washington, D.C., and Minneapolis. Becoming a U.S. citizen in 2010 was a proud moment for me, marking my commitment to this country while maintaining my Ghanaian roots. Living here has been challenging as an independent artist, but it has also given me opportunities to grow and share my music with diverse audiences.
cues: New York City, Washington D.C., Minneapolis
